draft.introduction: 1 Kings 3 recounts Solomon’s dream at Gibeon, where God offers him anything he desires. Solomon asks for wisdom to govern, and God grants it along with wealth and honor. The passage presents wisdom as the supreme divine gift. From the Septuagint.

translation.text: And the Lord appeared to Solomon in a dream by night, and the Lord said, “Ask what I should give you.” And Solomon said, “Give your servant a hearing heart to judge your people, to discern between good and evil; for who is able to judge this your great people?”
translation.original_text: καὶ ὤφθη κύριος τῷ Σαλωμων ἐν ὑπνῳ τὴν νύκτα

translation.text: And the word was pleasing before the Lord, that Solomon asked this thing. And God said to him, “Because you asked for this thing, and did not ask for yourself long life, and did not ask for riches, and did not ask for the lives of your enemies, but asked for yourself understanding to hear judgment—behold, I have done according to your word. I have given you a wise and understanding heart.”
translation.original_text: καὶ ἤρεσεν ἐνώπιον κυρίου ὅτι ἐζήτησεν Σαλωμων τὸ ὅῆμα τοῦτο

translator_notes.note: Solomon asks for a “hearing heart” (καρδίαν ἀκούειν), not merely intellectual knowledge. In Hebrew thought, the heart (lev) is the seat of understanding, will, and intention. True wisdom is receptive—a capacity to hear and discern rather than a store of information.
